Mellor-Yamada Level 2.5 Turbulence Closure  
 
1. Summary of the Algorithm 
 
This mixing model is described in detail in Mellor and Yamada (1982) and in the Princeton 
Ocean Model Users Guide (Mellor, 1998). To implement MY mixing in HYCOM, viscosity and 
scalar diffusivity are first parameterized as follows:

being a Richardson number. In these expressions, q  is the turbulence velocity scale, l  is the 
turbulence length scale, and r  is the potential density. The variables 2q  (turbulent kinetic 
energy) and 2ql  are prognostic variables in this model. The equations for these variables, written 
here as a function of the generalized vertical coordinate s  for the purpose of HYCOM

where qL  and lL  represent the sum of all local processes. Equations (5) and (6) have the same 
form as the equations for T  and S  in generalized vertical coordinates. Terms two through four 
in both equations represent horizontal advection, horizontal diffusion, and fluxes across the 
generalized vertical coordinates when these coordinates are relocated ( 0s ¹& ) by the hybrid 
coordinate adjustment algorithm. For T  and S , the local processes are surface forcing and 
vertical diffusivity. For 2q  and 2ql , the local processes are boundary forcing and vertical 
diffusivity plus three additional forcing and damping mechanisms described in the following 
section. 
 
2. Implementation Issues 
 
The strategy for implementing this turbulence closure model in HYCOM is to solve the same 
local equations for 2q  and 2ql  that are solved in POM. The purely local equations are

where qF  and lF  represent local boundary forcing and the remaining four terms of each equation 
represent vertical diffusion, generation by vertical shear, conversion to and from potential 
energy, and dissipation. The parameter ±W  is the wall proximity function given by 
± 21()WElkL=++ , with ( ) 111 ()LzHz h --- =-+- , where h is sea surface elevation 
and H  is water depth. The local solution procedure implemented in HYCOM is essentially the 
same one used in POM. Values of the several constants used in HYCOM are the same as those 
used in POM (see Mellor, 1998). 
Existing HYCOM algorithms are employed to calculate changes in 2q  and 2ql  due to the two 
nonlocal terms and the s&  term in (5) and (6). Horizontal advection and diffusion of 2q  and 2ql  
are performed in subroutine tsadvc.f, while the s&  flux terms are estimated in the hybrid vertical 
coordinate subroutine hybgen.f. Both 2q  and 2ql  are carried at the two leap frog time steps, and 
time smoothing is performed in the same manner as for other model layer variables. 
One significant problem had to be overcome to implement the algorithm in this manner. In the 
POM Mellor-Yamada algorithm, the variables 2q  and 2ql  are carried at vertical interfaces while 
HYCOM scalars are carried as layer variables. The strategy employed is to define a separate 
vertical coordinate system on which the one-dimensional solutions for 2q  and 2ql  are performed. 
This MY vertical coordinate system is illustrated alongside the HYCOM vertical coordinate 
system in the Figure. MY interfaces are located at the central depths of HYCOM model layers. 
There is one additional layer and one additional interface present in the MY coordinate system. 
With 2q  and 2ql  defined on MY interfaces, implementation of the POM local solution procedure 
in HYCOM is straightforward. Since MY interfaces are located at the central depths of HYCOM 
layers, values on MY interfaces are passed to the HYCOM routines that calculate the horizontal 
advection, horizontal diffusion, and 
s&  flux terms of (5) and (6). After these algorithms are 
executed, the layer values of 2q  and 2ql  are passed as interface values on the MY grid to the 
one-dimensional solution algorithm. When the one-dimensional routine is executed, HYCOM 
momentum and thermodynamical layer variables must be vertically interpolated to the MY 
layers. Since the central depth of each MY layer is located half way between the central depths 
of two HYCOM layers, the interpolated MY layer variables are calculated as the average values 
of these two HYCOM layers. 
Instead of employing the POM procedures to solve vertical diffusion equations for other 
model variable

catalog_entry.summary: This paper discusses the implementation of the Mellor-Yamada Level 2.5 turbulence closure model within the HYCOM ocean circulation model, detailing equations and methods for calculating turbulent kinetic energy and length scale.

variables.long_name: turbulent kinetic energy length scale product
